content:
First off: Hi, I'm Philipp and one of the maintainers of Clippy and I'm quite interested in this project for some time now, but just discovered the monthly community call and this Zulip last week :wave:

And now good news everyone! I managed to "talk" my employer Embecosm into giving me time to work on Rust-for-Linux this month (without external funding) :tada:

Since I'm free to work on pretty much anything that helps this project, I wanted to first ask what the most pressing issues on the rustc/Clippy side are? So if you would really like to see a specific feature to be worked on or are waiting for a bugfix etc., let me know and I will help you fix those :+1:

That's awesome! Welcome!

https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/issues/349 is the current tracking issue for things we want from clippy

Thanks! I already saw the tracking issues (also for the unstable Rust features). I'm happy to just work down that list. But I figured I'd ask first, so I spend my time in the most useful way for the project.
I think @Miguel Ojeda put together that list, he probably has opinions on relative priority.
That is great to hear Philipp! Please thank your company on our behalf!
On specific issues, I think https://github.com/rust-lang/rust-clippy/issues/7323 is currently the most interesting one to solve.
But, since you offered yourself, we will be on the lookout to give you more work (just kidding ;P).

On a more serious note, there is nothing very urgent at the moment; but as we discussed in the call, the best thing would be a way to write custom kernel lints -- but I realize that is probably outside of what your company allows you to work on.

So yeah, that linked issue is probably the most interesting one. The other one on normalizing semicolons is not a big deal, just a nice-to-have. The configuration file is not really needed either now that passing things in the command line seems to work (or always worked); so I could actually take it off the list, even.

Thanks for the overview! I'm happy to implement https://github.com/rust-lang/rust-clippy/issues/7323 :+1:

we will be on the lookout to give you more work (just kidding ;P)

Please do! If it should get too much I'll let you know. :) I'm also happy to work on rustc, not only Clippy.

the best thing would be a way to write custom kernel lints

Yeah, this is a bit too big of a task to fit into my allotted time. I thought about this a bit. As a short term workaround I thought about creating a template project, where you can get a quick start on writing lints with cargo-generate. With that I'd also get to know the pain points of writing lint tools and could then improve those in rustc, so that such a template is not required anymore. Do you think that would help?

The configuration file is not really needed either

Yeah I think that adding configuration files to rustc rather than cargo(/clippy) would be really hard to get approved, since rustc itself doesn't really have infrastructure for parsing configuration files AFAIK. I think all the configuration files in the Rust ecosystem are handled by cargo or the tools (rustfmt, clippy, ...), not rustc. But I could be missing something.

I'm also happy to work on rustc, not only Clippy.

For things other than Clippy, please see https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/issues/2 -- it is the features we use, but I keep there a list of links to other issues related to things we would like in rustc, rustdoc, alloc, etc.

Do you think that would help?

It may help, but I think as soon as we need to start a new project altogether, it is not important whether a template is there or not (of course, it may save time). @Gary Guo had a klint tool already started, so it may not help us much (i.e. that could be our "template", unless you are thinking of a more involved template with support to add easily lints etc.).

(I moved your messages to the correct topic)

as soon as we need to start a new project altogether

Ah do you mean that it should be possible to write lints as plugins to rustc, rather than writing a new tool?

In that case, this probably won't happen. Compiler plugins were deprecated a long time ago and are not likely to come back.

So writing a new tool to implement lints will probably always be the case. The only question would be how easy it is to write such a tool / how much boilerplate is required.

klint is already the right approach here. I see some general improvements for it. If you want, I can submit a PR towards klint. E.g. using #![register_tool(klint)], so you can namespace those lints and control them with #[allow(klint::lint_name)]. Ah wait this has to be done in the crate that uses that tool.

I'm working on a spinlock that preserves IRQ flags and I'm getting a clippy warning for a case when the return type of a function in a trait is determined by an associated type: when the associated type happens to be (), clippy complains, saying that I should omit the return type (and value). But given that I'm implementing a trait, I can't really omit them.

Here's a cutdown version of the problem: https://play.rust-lang.org/?version=stable&mode=debug&edition=2018&gist=cfc4d86e09daff73722c1c05ad1b2f9c (and a workaround that I don't think is great as using () directly is much clearer)

Could we improve this warning to exclude cases when we're implementing a trait?
Oh, it just occurred to me that omitting this doesn't really change the function type, so the clippy is warning is actually a good one!

I don't think we can make the suggestion more clear in this case. I added the "fixed" example to the lint documentation: https://github.com/rust-lang/rust-clippy/pull/7458 Maybe that helps in the future.

Thanks Philipp!

My problem was that I thought clippy was suggesting that I change the function type, which wasn't the case. I think mentioning that the two are _equivalent_, as you did, would have helped me realise this sooner.
